Here we go with definitions first. It does not harm even if they repeat. If you are familiar with these definitions, then you can skip to the main update following the five definitions below. CPU (Central Processing Unit): Remember the big desktop machines that had largely three parts — a display screen, keyboard/mouse, and a big box that we used to call a CPU. I am not referring to the big box, but to the main chip inside those big boxes and now in your laptops and smartphones. A CPU is a general-purpose processor that executes computer instructions, manages logic, control, and computation across diverse applications efficiently. GPU (Graphics Processing Unit): In 2012–13, when I was working for MSC Software, we had access to a GPU card from NVIDIA when we had no idea that this provider would become the world’s most valued company in 2025. The same GPU — with many advanced versions from that point on — is now used to accelerate large-scale mathematical computations in AI, namely the build-out of Large Language Models.

DPU (Data Processing Unit): NVIDIA also now builds and sells DPUs, which offload data movement, encryption, and virtualization tasks from CPUs for efficient cloud workloads. As such, both GPUs and DPUs work as co-processors or copilots to the CPUs.

TPU (Tensor Processing Unit): Google built this AI-specific processor to accelerate deep-learning matrix operations, optimizing performance for neural network training and inference.

QPU (Quantum Processing Unit): QPUs will be used to perform massively parallel probabilistic computations beyond classical limits. For now, just appreciate the reference to “probabilistic” and “quantum” contexts as these will be closely tied to the principles of quantum mechanics as leveraged in quantum computations.

The reason for mentioning these five processors is to appreciate that NVIDIA will play in the space of GPUs, DPUs, and QPUs in the long run. Google will continue evolving the TPU space. And the fact remains that CPUs have existed historically for a very, very long time — ever since the introduction of computers to mankind.

NVIDIA from 30 Nov 2022 to Now We will very soon celebrate the 3rd anniversary of OpenAI’s first ChatGPT released in Nov 2022. In these years, many Large Language Models from several software giants were announced. Hugging Face, one of the top model repositories, now hosts close to 2 million language models, of which there are more open-source models than closed-source models. Over these 3 years, NVIDIA shipped a series of GPUs and CPUs starting with the H100.

- It earned ~$61B of revenue in 2024, more than doubling from ~$23B in revenues in 2023.
- The company’s market capitalization was around $1T in mid-2023.
- In 2024, it pivoted to the Blackwell-era GPUs — B100 & B200 — designed to train trillion-parameter models.
- It generated ~$120B–$130B in revenues in the current year and the market capitalization grew from ~$3T in mid-2024 to close to ~$5T by late 2025.
- Cost differentials per unit GPU between the H100 and B100/B200 were insignificant; however, performance more than doubled with model parameters going from billions to trillions. Power consumption improved as well, given the superior architecture and interconnectivity in the Blackwell (B100/B200) series.

NVIDIA: Looking Forward — 2026 & 2027

- The next chapter starts with Rubin, its 2026 GPU platform, and Rubin Ultra set for 2027. These are successors to Blackwell and will train and run multi-trillion-parameter models at global scale.
- Compute capacity of Rubin will more than double from Blackwell — similar to the move from H100 to Blackwell.
- Rubin GPUs will be 2x better than Blackwell for computing and will connect to DPUs to handle data traffic so that they can focus purely on AI math.
- The networking capabilities will allow hundreds of GPUs to work together across multiple racks as a single coherent supercomputer.
- Rubin Ultra will have better power efficiencies and will support massive “AI factories” consuming gigawatts of power — efficiently.
- Revenue will surpass $200B by 2026 and valuation or market capitalization will hover between $4T–$5T.

Directional Eye Charts (Narrative) To simplify the narrative for larger absorption — and to set up the questions I have — here are the directional charts I am referring to. Think of them in 3 halves each for 2023, 2024, and 2025 (H1 ’23, H2 ’23, H1 ’24, H2 ’24, H1 ’25, H2 ’25):

1. Release of NVIDIA GPU capabilities over the last 3 years 2. Major LLMs released in the same duration 3. NVIDIA’s half-yearly achieved revenue for the same 6 periods 4. NVIDIA’s revenue-to-valuation multiplier 5. NVIDIA’s market capitalization for the same period — and a compare/contrast to global GDP for 2025 at ~$118T 6. Market capitalization of the Mag 7 in the same 6 phases 7. Estimated global AI revenue (a pure-play estimate at a max of $0.9T global number) This is the heart of the tale of (one) city — the AI-and-accelerators city. The Questions That Matter

- Is the growth in these massive valuations realized? (Or is it still largely in “expected future flows” and discounted-back narratives?)
- Have we gained real momentum in utilization of these platforms and technologies? (Inference workloads, agentic workloads, AI in production, AI in the edge, not just LLM playgrounds.)
- Is the growth in global GDP from the AI revolution — or is it displacement of existing GDP to AI platforms and hyperscaler ecosystems — and is that justified?

I have more thoughts from another research stream to show impacts on employment, financial markets, supply chains, and production systems. I will write about that as the second city. Thank you for reading along so far.

title: The Tale of the Second City: AI's Gold Rush?
content:
Here is the link to the missing charts from two foiled attachment attempts from the last week.

Here is The Tale of the Second City. When Snowflake’s stock opened in September 2020 at $245, with the company’s valuation reaching nearly $68 billion, it marked the world’s largest software IPO valuation ever. In contradiction, during the last week, NVIDIA’s stock dropped to $188 (at $200 on Monday, 10 Nov 2025) from the peak of $212 a piece. This drop in a week represented a decline of more than $450 billion in the company’s valuation — a fall more than six times the valuation of the world’s largest software IPO. This is the see-saw the American stock market is experiencing. Amidst trade and tariff tensions, the valuations still remain very bullish, largely driven by AI optimism. This other city anticipates a worry that we are going to experience a huge, painful market correction. The recent drop in the market cannot be ascribed to specific things; however, here are some opinions that matter.

Michael Burry’s Scion Asset Management disclosed massive put or short positions against Palantir and NVIDIA worth a combined $1.1 billion in their regulatory filing. They essentially bet that both NVIDIA and Palantir’s stocks are overvalued and will drop. This is the same Michael Burry on whom the movie The Big Short was based.

In a recent podcast appearance with Brad Gerstner of Altimeter Capital, Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, grew visibly frustrated when asked how his company could justify projecting $1.4 trillion in infrastructure investment while generating only about $13 billion in annual revenue. Imagine a revenue that is less than 0.1% of the potential infrastructure investment. As life would have it, ironically, the details of this fact were revealed through my request to ChatGPT.

In another report, Geeta Gopinath, ex-Chief Economist and Deputy Managing Director of the International Monetary Fund, shared her concerns about a potential market crash — one that could be far more severe and global in scope compared to the dotcom crash a quarter century ago. The world, and particularly American households, have been highly enthusiastic about the U.S. stock market — encouraged by strong returns, dominant American tech firms (Mag 7 and more), and the U.S. dollar’s status as the world’s reserve currency.

The potential of this correction could reduce the market by over $20 trillion domestically — roughly 70% of American GDP in 2024 — and by $15 trillion globally in non-American representation.

In short, the awkwardness that Sam Altman demonstrated in discussing investment, revenue, and valuation at OpenAI, the shorting of NVIDIA and Palantir by Michael Burry, and Geeta Gopinath’s warnings do not indicate that one should sell everything tomorrow. However, the risks are real, the stakes are high, and complacency is misplaced.

So — What Are You Doing About It?

title: Telescope or Microscope
content:
Every so often, you meet someone who proudly proclaims, “I don’t get paid to do that.” This thinking is the opposite of what fuels great careers, great teams, and great organizations. So let’s borrow two magnificent tools from science to rewire how we look at work. On one side: the James Webb Space Telescope, scanning galaxies 13 billion years away and expanding our sense of what’s possible. On the other: nanotechnology, operating at the scale of molecules, proving that the smallest moves can transform entire industries. One widens imagination; the other sharpens precision. In today’s world—be it corporate, entrepreneurial, artistic, medical, or professional—we need both.

But mindset transformation begins with choosing the telescope first.

1. The Telescoping View: Seeing Beyond the Ordinary Zoom out far enough, and your job description becomes a footnote—useful, but hardly the full story. A telescopic mindset helps you recognize how your role connects across the constellation of people: customers, colleagues, partners, society, and the future you are shaping. With that view, even everyday tasks become mission-linked.

- A developer isn’t checking in code; they’re contributing to reliability that drives revenue.
- A cashier isn’t just scanning items; they’re shaping a moment that defines whether a customer returns
- A physician isn’t simply diagnosing; they’re restoring someone’s ability to return to life, family, and purpose Through the telescope, lines in a job description blur, and horizons of impact sharpen. 2. Doing More Than What’s Asked — For the Right Intent Doing more is not about burnout, martyrdom, or being the “office hero.” It’s about thoughtful contributions rooted in the bigger mission. When you act with telescopic awareness, your bar rises naturally.
- You preempt risks instead of reacting.
- You improve inefficient processes because they slow down everyone, not because someone told you to.
- You step into gaps because you care about outcomes, not because your title authorizes it.

This isn’t overwork. It’s ownership.

People who think this way grow faster, lead earlier, and build reputational equity that outlasts any role. They aren’t valuable because they do more—they’re valuable because they see more. 3. The Five Lenses: The CORE+ Framework To simplify the five behaviors into something memorable, here’s a five-letter acronym: CORE+. Each letter captures a mindset that transcends titles, industries, seniority, and professions. C — Customer-Cognitive Mindset Think like the customer you are serving. Anticipate their needs, interpret what they truly want, and design your actions around their experience. The best professionals read the room, the data, and the unstated expectations.

O — Ownership for Value & Growth Everything you touch should improve something: revenue, trust, efficiency, safety, reliability, or skill. People with an ownership mindset don’t wait to be assigned value—they create it. R — Results Through Product Thinking Whatever you produce—a report, a legal brief, a diagnosis, a marketing plan, a service interaction—is a “product” of your thinking. Treat it like an artifact the world will use and judge. Quality becomes your signature.

E — Excellence in Service Orientation Service is not subservience. It’s clarity about why your role exists: to enable someone else’s outcome. Leaders, creators, analysts, physicians, engineers—all serve someone. When service becomes a mindset, not a function, your influence multiplies.

+ — Culture Creation The plus sign symbolizes your force multiplier. Culture is shaped by micro-behaviors: how you respond under stress, how you treat colleagues, how you share credit, how you listen, and how you show up. People who elevate culture uplift entire systems.

Together, CORE+ forms the foundation of working beyond the narrow boundaries of “what’s written in my job description.” 4. Redefining Monday — and the New Year Ahead Here’s the truth: the gap between average and exceptional is measured in tiny adjustments, not massive reinventions. Your next Monday starts with a telescopic question:

“What’s the bigger mission my role contributes to?”

### Then a microscopic one

“What single action today can elevate value, culture, or customer experience?”

### Your new Monday might involve

- 
- 
- 
- 
- closing one cross-functional gap that no one owns, improving one broken process, helping one colleague without prompting, listening to one customer more deeply, driving one behavior that strengthens CORE+.

Live this every week, and your new year resolution becomes self-fulfilling:

Expand your field of vision, shrink your excuses.

My next piece will focus on the art of acting without explicit instructions—building a super-cognitive awareness of the world around you. Today was the telescope; next time, we will pick up the microscope.

title: Microscope: Seeing the Mind
content:
MICROSCOPE: Seeing the Mind, Not the Minute Details After learning to look at the world through the telescope — expanding imagination, connecting dots, rising above the walls of job descriptions — the natural next move is to reach for a different instrument: the microscope. Or better still, its mischievous cousin, the nanoscope. If the telescope trains the eyes, the microscope trains the mind.

Let me begin with a story.

Years ago, early in my executive leadership journey, a newly hired employee pulled me aside, visibly annoyed. Her manager, in her words, had an “excruciating need to check every task daily — sometimes twice.” The constant nudging, reviewing, and hovering frustrated her so much that she sarcastically upgraded his title. Not micromanager. Nomanager. Nanomanager. Scientifically accurate. Comedically lethal.

I laughed.

And then I reflected.

This is exactly what happens when leaders confuse “detail orientation” with “daily interrogation.” When they replace “support” with “surveillance.” When organizations mistake “visibility” for “vigilance.” Micromanagement of time and tasks doesn’t create excellence; it manufactures exhaustion. It doesn’t help people fly; it trims their wings.

What people actually need is clarity, direction, and trust — and then the freedom to experiment, struggle a little, fail safely, recover, and grow.

Deep dives and detail orientation are powerful when they help someone solve a problem — not when they become the problem. Immersive analysis makes sense in research, scientific inquiry, debugging, diagnosis, or legal strategy. But human-centric work demands a different touch: passion without pressure, involvement without intrusion, guidance without gripping. So, in this leadership chapter, the microscope is not for zooming into calendars, keystrokes, or minute-by-minute accounting. It is for zooming into how the mind works — yours and theirs. Which brings me to a framework I created years ago during a leadership program: the ACK Model.

ACK: A Mind-Reading Model (The Healthy Kind) Anyone who grew up reading Amar Chitra Katha will remember the magic of those speech and thought bubbles — the bold ones for dialogue, the dotted ones for inner truth. Real life is the opposite: thoughts are invisible unless you train your mind to see them.

### ACK builds that muscle through three steps

A — Awareness of Your Own Thinking Before decoding others, understand your own wiring.

How do you process ambiguity?

What triggers assumptions or shortcuts?

This self-awareness is the base for personal and professional scale.

C — Comprehension of How Others Think This is the real microscope.

Not what people say — but how they think.

Not what leaders direct — but how they reason.

Not what customers ask — but what shapes their ask.

When you understand how people think, your dependence on explicit instruction evaporates. You stop waiting for clarity; you begin creating it.

K — Knowledge of Ecosystems and Influences A doctor knows patients arrive with Google-based diagnoses.

A lawyer knows clients walk in with WhatsApp legal theories.

A realtor knows buyers carry decades of second-hand opinions.

When you understand the ecosystem of biases, fears, misinformation, and motivations, you become more empathetic, more effective, and more credible.

ACK is mind-reading anchored in integrity — curiosity without manipulation. Microscope vs. Nanoscope: The Real Message Microscope leadership is not about controlling details; it is about spotting invisible patterns. Nanoscope leadership is not about diving deeper into tasks; it is about diving deeper into cognition.

The point is not to obsess.

The point is to understand.

Understand yourself.

Understand others.

Understand the environment you operate in.

When you do, something interesting happens: you no longer need explicit directions to perform. Job descriptions shrink into footnotes — not because you zoomed out with the telescope, but because you zoomed into the mind with the microscope.

A Closing Thought Think about how you think.

Learn how others think.

Act with clarity, not confusion.

The microscope is not a tool of scrutiny; it is a tool of awareness.

And when you pair the telescope for your eyes with the microscope for your mind, your potential no longer fits inside any job description.

title: Benchmarking the Art of the (Im)possible
content:
We have heard that we are the average of the five people closest to us. Though it sounds relatively simple and comforting, it is dangerously incomplete. More on why? We have strange habits when it comes to benchmarks. We benchmark by wealth, wisdom, character and most importantly by accomplishments. The implicit mind also keeps underperformers close, not out of compassion, but convenience. We can tell them, advise them and flaunt our being in front of them. They act as our emotional mirrors, reassuring us that we are doing fine and better than them. It is like buying dopamine for free leading to temporary happiness and zero growth. This sometimes gets into lazy benchmarking. Also, your mind does not know that this is happening. We confuse comfort with progress. And then, we wonder why years pass without significant progress.

High achievers, in contradiction, work differently. They benchmark against people who make them uncomfortable. They study those who prepare more obsessively, persevere longer when motivation fades, and practice even when no one is watching. They pay attention not just to what is delivered, but how it is wrapped—the storytelling, the packaging, the ability to move ideas from raw intelligence to compelling narratives.

This is where the shift happens—from the art of the possible to the art of the impossible. This shift begins with choosing better benchmarks.

Let me share a real example. It's 2:00 a.m. in India. Middle of the week. The night of his birthday. Tarun just turned 25. His evening moving into late night would have been like loud music, drinks galore, and tomorrow written off in advance.

At 2 a.m., Tarun is not sloshed. He was inquisitive. He was asking questions that most people don't ask until their late thirties—if ever. I was in New York and he along with his teammate found me since it was a convenient time for me to be available. He spent the hour with me speaking about preparation, perseverance, and practice—not as motivational words, but as systems. He was dissecting how high performers think, how they tell their stories, how they raise standards quietly and relentlessly. On his birthday. That is not a coincidence—that is benchmarking.

Tarun isn't measuring himself against the average 25-year-old. He's benchmarking against a future version of excellence—and working backwards.

Are your benchmarks making you comfortable—or making you better?

The 4Ps: A Framework for Upward Benchmarking High achievers, consciously or otherwise, benchmark across four dimensions. Let's call them the 4Ps.

1. Preparation: Preparation is invisible work—you show up early, leave late, and compound quietly. People who benchmark upward obsess over inputs, not outcomes. They simulate scenarios before they occur. My favorite with that is from the years of learning from Dominic Gallello, my favorite leader. You walk into his meetings expecting the unexpected. Average performers prepare just enough to survive. High performers prepare enough to expect the unexpected.

2. Perseverance: Perseverance is misunderstood as grit. It's not. Rather, the benchmark shifts from "Can I do this?" to "Can I stay with this longer than others?"

3. Packaging (The Forgotten Multiplier): Ideas don't travel unless they're packaged. Storytelling is not fluff—it's leverage. The best performers study how ideas are framed, not just formed. They watch how leaders simplify complexity, how narratives are built, how timing and tone matter.

4. Possibility → Impossibility: This is the final leap. Most benchmarks live in the realm of the possible: realistic goals, incremental growth, sensible ambition. Necessary—but insufficient. The great benchmarks operate in the realm of the impossible, the 10x thinking and the force multiplier.

This trait alone separates followers from hallmarks. Do you have implicit bias for comforting benchmarks? Have you thought of your 10x goals? Do your biases prevent you from 10x expectations?

title: Is Your Life a Netflix Revenge Series?
content:
No Enemies, Only Elevation: Most always our implicit thinking is about how we are seen and how we want to respond to get back at something. We think with the ’tit for tat’ mind, a scorekeeping behavior or retaliatory reciprocation. Someone ignores you, you ignore them back. Someone blocks you, you return the favor—emotionally, socially, professionally. It feels fair and balanced, however it is deeply unproductive.

Your competition is not with people, but standards. Standards of thinking, standards of behavior, standards of growth - the standards you set for yourself. Families, friendships, teams, and organizations are not battlegrounds; they are arenas where we either elevate each other or slowly decay through scorekeeping.

At work, tit-for-tat shows up quietly. A colleague doesn’t credit you in a meeting, so you withhold information next time. A manager challenges your idea publicly, so you disengage privately. The relationship becomes transactional, brittle, and small. The irony? The real competition—market forces, smarter startups, faster technologies—don’t care about your internal cold war. At home and socially, the pattern repeats. “I called last time.” “They didn’t show up for me.” Relationships turn into spreadsheets. But human bonds don’t compound through arithmetic; they compound through generosity and context. Often, people are not against you—they are simply dealing with something you cannot see.

Wearing the other Person’s Shoes: Which brings us to a critical muscle most of us undertrain: seeing what you don’t see. Our cognitive biases love stories about heroes, villains, success, failures, and glamour. Reality is messier. We tend to operate with partial information, hidden pressures, and expectations for outcomes, eventually to realize we did not get what we expected in the first place. Expecting the unexpected isn’t pessimism; it’s maturity. When you pause and ask, “What might I be missing?” you dissolve conflicts before they harden. This matters even more today because many of our “rooms” are invisible. They are not conference rooms but video tiles, delayed replies, or just a voice on a call across time zones. Tone collapses in emails. We often misread responses or lack thereof as resistance. Watercooler conversations are even more dangerous. Hearsay from a third person—often incomplete—cements opinions before direct engagement. Reading an invisible room requires slowing down, listening for what isn’t said, and checking assumptions early—before imaginary enemies form.

This needs a fix of proximity—to influential people. Not influential by title alone, but by gravity. People who raise the quality of conversation, think in systems, and stay calm under pressure. You find them by observing who others turn to when things go wrong. You approach them not with asks, but with curiosity.

So how do we start afresh in 2026? Rewire your minds with a long-game logic. Set a very high bar and standards, somewhat hyperbolically impossible to achieve. Work hard to live up to your high bar expectations Assume positive intent until proven otherwise—and even then, respond with clarity, not retaliation. Widen your lens. Be deliberate about the rooms you enter, visible or invisible. Choose circles that stretch you.

Once you can commit to wire your mind to this thinking, you can set functional or performance goals.

title: AI Won't Steal Jobs — Leaders Will
content:
AI will not steal jobs: Leaders will, by replacing learning with automation Over the last two weeks, amidst the festivities, my spirits were knocked down a few times by impacts from global disorders emerging from geopolitical, technological, and economic instabilities — largely arising from the AI world.

We have seen the rise and fall of economic and technological bubbles starting with the dotcom era before the turn of the century. The dotcom bubble created fictional multi-million-dollar companies almost overnight. We then saw technological reshaping through mobile and smart devices, the migration to cloud-based data centers, social media growth, and now the AI cycle. It is hard to decipher both the economic and technological hype, as well as the time it will take for the AI cycle to materialize. AI, at face value, sounds like it will solve the world hunger crisis however, we are not there today. The more I have played with AI, the more it is making me (us) realize that even implementing AI takes time. This is a process that necessitates education, identifying your technology stack, protecting the enterprise, building proofs-of-concept, and then building actual real-life use cases — beyond simply showcasing abilities.

LLMs have been equated to buying gas (petrol) from gas stations, with token costs from providers compared to choosing which pump offers the lowest price. The LLM users still need to build a comprehensive wrapping solution to effectively capitalize model capabilities, compounded by the non-deterministic nature of LLMs. Models respond with different answers to the same question asked by multiple users at the same time, or different answers to the same question asked by the same user at different points in time.

While we have all appreciated these unpragmatic patterns, there is a bigger problem surfacing in our world, which largely led to my spirits getting knocked down. What do I mean? Let’s unfold. Immigration posture in developed economies — largely that of the United States — has created significant economic uncertainty. Aspiring international students are finding it hard to get employed. This unemployment challenge extends to all aspiring graduates, immigrating or naturalized, who have spent four to six rigorous academic years and up to a few hundred thousand dollars on education. The advent of AI in addition to headwinds from interest rates, headwinds from macroeconomic uncertainties and overhiring in the recent years have significantly impacted creation of entry level jobs. The machines (computers) are doing what were once considered as entry level jobs. Here are some critical questions to answer:

- If new college graduates are not entering the industry, how will the ones exiting get backfilled if there is no pipeline?
- If AI creates more socialistic outcomes for planetary equitable payouts, will the disproportionate incomes generated by technology companies lead to higher taxes to fund society at large?
- Market Capitalization growth in the United States is largely represented by AI companies (Magnificent 7 vs. 493 others in S&P 500, for example). Does this mean the voice of these AI companies will supersede the needs of ordinary citizens over time?
- Is this the last phase before political and electoral influence from citizens truly matters?
- Should we not be more worried about AI than immigration issues?
- If AI performs cognitive labor for less than minimum wage, increased fuel needs to run AI data centers will drive higher energy costs, while identities and security face greater vulnerabilities. Are large-scale backlashes and societal impacts being adequately put in perspective?
- Are policymakers cognitively & actively planning to defend these challenges? What will major economies do to defend against this compelling situation created on the back of a capitalistic society?
- Am I writing this, and are you reading it, because we are somewhat protected by a monetary safety net?

While all these questions remain unanswered, we need to do our part. We need to figure out ways to employ the youth. Instead of eliminating interns who get replaced by AI, we need to bring the youth back into employment — to educate, manage, curate, and audit our AI world. This is an existential crisis, and we all have a role to play.

title: Markets Are Rich, Society Isn't
content:
Markets Are Rich, Society Isn’t: AI, Paper Wealth, and the New Divide Let’s examine the see-saw of AI, Wealth, Gold and the Governments. For the last two weeks, I have been making an attempt to write about this and quite coincidentally, Vox Markets website tagged the headline below.

“London open: Stocks fall on Trump tariff threats but defence firms, gold miners shine” AI: A Two-Speed Reality: Over the last 24 months, the US economy has quietly split into two parallel worlds. A small cluster of AI-heavy giants is pulling indices upward with disproportionate force, while the remaining hundreds of companies behave like a mature economy should: modest growth, margin pressure, cautious hiring, and incremental innovation. AI is no longer just a technology wave—it is the primary growth engine of the US economy. This creates a structural tug of war. Markets celebrate AI-driven productivity gains, while the real economy absorbs the lag. The risk isn’t an AI bubble—it’s an AI imbalance. The question ahead isn’t whether AI will win—but whether the rest of the economy can catch up before patience, capital, or labor runs out.

AI: Growth Without Broad Prosperity Most AI growth has come from infrastructure—chips, data centers, cloud capacity, energy contracts, and capital-heavy platforms. This is foundational investment, not downstream economic expansion. With AI, we are building the highways, not yet seeing traffic. AI today is improving margins for a few firms, not creating net-new demand across the economy. Meanwhile, the stock market surge primarily rewards capital, not labor. Gains are increasingly concentrated among asset holders—the top 10%, perhaps even narrower. Equity appreciation looks impressive on screens, but it does little for households navigating rising rents, expensive mortgages, fragile employment, and stubborn living costs.

AI: A Revolution Feared Before It Pays Back What makes this cycle different is the emotional response. AI may be the most feared technological revolution in modern history. The dominant narrative is not opportunity but displacement—jobs lost, skills obsoleted, and purpose questioned. The prevailing anxiety isn’t “what can we build?” but “what happens to us?” Earlier revolutions—steam engines, electricity, automobiles, even the rise of cloud computing technologies disrupted labor, but didn’t directly challenge cognitive work at scale. AI does. This fear precedes ROI and does not replace it. I am an optimist however, meaningful economic impact—measured in sustained productivity gains, new job categories, and lower unit costs—likely sits two to three years out. Even that timeline is uncertain.

Moving from AI to Wealth: AI’s impact does not stop at productivity or fear; it spills directly into how wealth is created, valued, and distributed.

Wealth, Where It Sits— Fragility of Equity Heavy Tilt Globally, wealth still rests on tangible foundations. Real estate dominates, accounting for roughly 45–55% of total global wealth, followed by financial assets—equity and debt combined—at about 40–45%. Gold, collectibles, and crypto, despite outsized attention, remain marginal in percentage terms. This structure reflects a world where land, housing, and long-lived assets anchor value.

The United States, however, tells a different story. Real estate represents closer to 30% of wealth, while financial assets account for roughly 37%—about $65 trillion of an estimated $175 trillion total. Equity plays an outsized role, but it is unevenly distributed, heavily concentrated among the top deciles. Insurance and pension assets add another ~19%, acting as a stabilizing—but slow-moving—counterweight.

This brings us to the uncomfortable question: how much of today’s wealth is tangible, and how much is confidence dressed up as valuation?

This equity-heavy tilt matters because much of today’s wealth is conditional. Price-to-free-cash-flow and forward revenue multiples suggest expectations are doing more work than fundamentals. When valuations rely on future perfection rather than present cash generation, wealth exists as long as confidence holds. Paper wealth isn’t imaginary—but it is reversible, capable of evaporating without GDP ever signaling distress.

When confidence in equity-heavy wealth begins to wobble, history shows investors instinctively look for anchors—gold being the oldest of them.

What about the Gold and the new Gold Rush?

Gold offers perspective, not replacement. At roughly $30 trillion, it represents about 6% of global wealth; in the U.S., official and private holdings together amount to barely ~0.6% of total wealth. Even dramatic gold revaluation cannot practically substitute for today’s equity and real-estate stack. Wealth, ultimately, is less about stores of value—and more about where belief, capital, and productivity intersect.

In the end, no asset class operates in a vacuum; governments shape the rules, the incentives, and the fears that markets respond to.

Government God Fearing Society: Traditionally, Asian society feared the government at large. This fear is experienced in the US now with how tariffs will drive or slow down economic growth of a given geography.

In today’s environment, fear of policy often moves markets more than policy itself, altering investment behavior, capital mobility, and economic momentum in advance.

### In a Nutshell

- AI growth has led to disproportionate growth of valuations in AI companies and traditional businesses continue to have growth, margins, talent and innovation pressures
- Equity valuations do not represent societal wealth. It is only a representation of disproportionate growth leading to increasing disparities
- Market wealth are good measures for GDP and indices however they are poor proxies for societal health
- Gold will be structurally insignificant as an asset class especially as a representation in the US economy. We cannot afford to give it a ride we gave to cryptos
- I said this before: Governments need to support capitalism, manage employment and enable growth. Prioritize regulations over immigration, occupation over disproportionate growth As an individual, I can only encourage you to have the cognizance of the changing landscape with AI, opportunities lost/gained, disproportionality of wealth, myth of gold and to observe how governments play a role to protect sanity of humankind.

title: The Air Is Toxic, and So Is Some Leadership
content:
This is my experience around how I traded a snowstorm in New York for polluted air in Mumbai—and learned something uncomfortable about leadership. The North-East—and a large part of the US—is experiencing inclement weather conditions. Before arriving in Mumbai yesterday, I thought I had escaped the snowstorm in New York, only to realize that I was simultaneously moving from an AQI of 25 to an AQI of 200+. Why does this matter? My colleague Amit and I were moving around in Mumbai, and I wore a mask throughout the day due to the high AQI. Wearing a mask for prolonged periods is not fun. While it protects you from particulate matter, it can also feel suffocating. As I walked with the mask on, it struck me how difficult it is for humans to control two things that involve the mouth: eating and talking—let alone talking while eating.

Over the last 10+ years, I’ve trained both hunger control and speech control through intermittent fasting and selective communication. Keeping dietary choices aside for a moment, let’s talk about communication.

When you speak unreasonably, out of turn, or unknowingly condescend, you lose authenticity. Active listening and selective speaking are core characteristics of authentic communication. This reflection reminded me of the “Tape Man,” who reached the finals of America’s Got Talent and now performs in Las Vegas—without uttering a single word throughout his extraordinary career trajectory.

There is a powerful lesson here: knowing when to communicate, how to express, and when not to speak at all. This is the power of selective communication—where, when you finally speak, attention naturally follows, regardless of the seat you hold at the table. So where is the storm in all of this?

Lately, I’ve seen many of us go through challenging career passages. If you know me, we’ve likely spoken about career storms—and sometimes personal ones. When you’re inside a storm, a twister of uncertainty, your composure and decorum matter the most. How you maintain your Hallo matters even more.

Much like “hangry” describes how hunger hijacks your mood, Hallo refers to how your hello to the world creates your halo. This applies whether you are already in a leadership role or building toward one. People read you—constantly—and form opinions.

Let’s talk about leaders specifically.

When a leader walks in, she creates a Hallo of either cheer or fear. Are people respecting your authority—or being influenced by your authenticity? Many leaders miss this mark. They turn their heads, trying to see what their halo looks like, forgetting that the halo is always behind them.

The only way to see your halo is to look into the mirror.

And not the kind of mirror that asks, “Mirror, mirror on the wall, who is the fairest of them all?” So what’s your Hello (Halo)? Do you bring cheer—or fear? Do people experience your wisdom—or your daftness?

If you’re unsure, don’t ask your peasants. Ask a child. She will tell you—honestly—whether the emperor is wearing clothes. Reflect and appreciate what your Authenticity Quality Index is like this is what you create and determine if people breathe fresh air or suffocate around you.

title: From Potential to Proof: Markets Reprice AI
content:
From the peak the global equity, Mag 7s, metals and crypto markets gained, we experienced a huge crash in the last week. Here is the recap of the blood bath across the board:

- $500B wiped off from the US Equity markets representing about 0.8% in the valuation
- $200B wiped off from Mag 7 representing almost 1% in the valuation
- $2.5T wiped off from Global Equity markets representing 1.5% in the valuation Although these valuations in % representations don’t show a significant order, the reality of technology, SaaS or AI first companies is slightly different. Here are some specific cases in point Sticker Current Drop Current Valuation $B AMZN $210 $33 $2,258.00 $355 $258 13.6 18.6 $516 GOOG $323 $21 $3,909.00 $254 $350 6.1 7.7 $327 MSFT $401 $23 $2,981.00 $171 $555 5.4 27.7 $1,145 META $661 $52 $1,673.00 $132 $796 7.3 17.0 $342 CRM $191 $20 $1,822.00 $191 $331 9.5 42.3 $1,335 SNOW $168 $24 $58.00 $8 $281 12.5 40.2 $39 ORCL $143 $22 $410.00 $63 $345 13.3 58.6 $579 WDAY $163 $11 $43.00 $3 $281 6.1 42.0 $31 TSLA $411 $15 $1,543.00 $56 $499 3.5 17.6 $330 SAP $203 $4 $237.00 $5 $313 1.9 35.1 $128 NOW $101 $19 $106.00 $19 $211 15.5 52.1 $115 Valuation Drop $B $1,257 Peak (52 Week) % Drop in a Week % Drop from Peak Value Drop from Peak $B $4,888 It is evident that these companies lost almost $5T from peak and $1.2T in the last week itself. So what caused these losses?

Outlook of SaaS and AI relevant software companies is feared by disruption from AI capabilities. This led to a big selloff in the tech sector with most Mag 7 and similar companies losing anywhere from 4 to 15% last week. Labor Market data put more pressure with 108,000 jobs lost in January, highest displacement loss since 2009. Answer this: “Is AI fear causing layoffs, or are layoffs amplifying AI fear?” This loss of confidence will dilute further as the valuation from potential growth will disappear owing to the fear of AI capabilities. These companies will need to build a moat:

- Demonstrating immediate realization of value for their customers, not the potential anymore
- Augmenting capabilities through organic product innovation and inorganic capability acquisition
- Enabling AI first narrative in their product offerings
- Building scale from providing controlled access to your data and capabilities to the ecosystem companion companies
- Lastly, significantly enabling and monitoring the customer success from capability deployment. Think about forward deployed engineers 2026 will see a lot more volatility. Valuations of non-SaaS technology companies based on potential will also be questioned through the year. The era of being rewarded for potential is ending. The era of being judged on delivery has begun. Valuations will follow execution, not imagination. In this cycle, execution is no longer a differentiator—it is the price of relevance, whether it is the market or you.

title: The Periodic Table of People
content:
At some stage in high school, you were introduced to the Periodic Table of Elements; matter organized by increasing atomic numbers. Move left to right and atomic weight increases. Properties change. Some elements react violently. Some bond easily. At the far right sit the noble gases which are inert, stable, and untouchable. From reactive Hydrogen to composed Helium.

Let’s borrow that logic and apply it to wealth.

In chemistry, elements are arranged by atomic weight, valence behavior, state (solid, liquid, gas), and stability. Now imagine a spectrum of wealth arranged the same way. Valuation Spectrum (Atomic Weight Equivalent)

- Light Elements (Left Side): Physical currency — M0 money — roughly $9T globally, about 2% of ~$450T global wealth. Next comes Gold at ~$14T, around 3% of global wealth. Surprisingly small, isn’t it? The assets we emotionally gravitate toward are numerically light.
- Heavy Elements: Government bonds (~$130T, ~30%). Equities (~$110T, ~25%). Real estate (~$300T, nearly two-thirds of global wealth). Real estate dominates the table. If wealth had gravity, property would bend space-time.
- Ultra-Heavy Elements: Global intellectual capital is represented by ideas, patents, algorithms, brand equity. Massive. Hard to measure. Increasingly dominant.
- Heaviest (Far Right): Health. Mental well-being. Composure. We say “health is wealth,” yet most portfolios don’t reflect that truth. If this were a true periodic table, mental stability would sit in the noble column with inertness and stability.

Reactivity Across the Spectrum

- Highly Reactive: Crypto, Shorts/Puts as speculative equity and NFTs have been highly reactive
- Moderately Reactive: Publicly traded equities and real-estate react moderately over time
- Stable Wealth: Gold and Sovereign bonds have demonstrated stability. One could argue that similar to environmental health, gold and bonds as representative wealth have become moderately reactive as well
- Noble Wealth: Mental well-being and composure are meant to be stable, at least as necessitated.

States of Matter

- 
- 
- 
- Solids: Cash, gold, property, antiques are tangible and grounded.

Liquids: Bonds, equities, crypto are transferable and fluid.

Gaseous: Intellect, brand, reputation are invisible but expansive.

Plasma (Life Force): Physical well-being and consciousness power everything else.

### In the chart below, observe carefully

- 
- 
- 
- Abstraction increases from left to right.

Tangibility decreases.

Stability increases at the far right.

Existential importance increases at the far right.

Yet where do we spend most of our time? Fighting daily battles on the left accumulating reactive, measurable wealth while aspiring to exist peacefully on the right. Life becomes a tug-of-war between accumulation and composure.

The irony? The elements at the far right cannot be bought with the ones on the left. They must be cultivated. So ask yourself: are you building atomic weight, or are you building noble stability?

title: The Path You Never Planned
content:
Author Marshall Goldsmith in the book titled, ‘What got you here, won’t get you there’ explains how successful people sabotage their growth through annoying habits.

The fact of the matter is that most of us aren’t where we are because we carefully mapped the route to be here, exactly speaking. We just simply landed here. You were doing a job and that became a career, skill on the job became your expertise and the opportunity landed early in the career became your identity. We convinced ourselves that was the plan all along when it actually wasn’t.

I don’t want anyone to question one's own integrity or commitments towards her work or question another accomplished leader’s path to success because irrespective of knowing or not knowing the path we chose, there will be a strong sense of curiosity, presence of luck, instincts to survive and a mindset of perseverance.

Of course, there is a minority of leaders who knew where they were going

### and here are some examples

Jeff Bezos left a comfortable Wall Street job because he believed the internet would transform commerce. His path wasn’t random — he wanted to build the “everything store,” and every major Amazon decision followed that direction.

Elon Musk has repeatedly articulated his destination: electrification of transport, multi-planetary civilization, and energy independence. Whether one agrees with him or not, the clarity of destination shaped his actions.

Satya Nadella reshaped Microsoft by focusing on a cloud-first world. He didn’t simply optimize what existed; he redirected the company toward where technology was heading. These leaders did not just succeed, they knew the game they were playing.

And, there is majority of us who did not know where we were going There were difficulties in the past of not knowing where we were intending to land and where we actually landed. Similarly, if you take today not only you do not know what will get you there but also that we do not know what ‘there’ actually means. We just exist because: there are necessities to exist, to prove, to have financial success, to have meaningful occupation for the next decade or two and most importantly to have a story for next generations, for your family or for the functions you are responsible for.

A lot of us can be termed successful, however we did not know where we were going. You have a disciplined operating model, even without a defined destination. You learn relentlessly, you are open to new opportunities, you exhibit intellectual curiosity and recognize the required momentum.

A Simple Framework for Choosing Actions If you don’t know the final destination, decisions must still be made every day. One way to

### prioritize choices is to ask

Which action has the highest potential impact?

### A practical hierarchy might look like this

1. 10× disruption or innovation 2. Revenue growth 3. Customer growth 4. Cost reduction 5. Time efficiency 6. Experiential improvement If an opportunity sits near the top of this list, it deserves attention. If it sits near the bottom, it may simply be noise. This lens helps prioritize decisions even when the long-term path is uncertain.

Discipline When the Destination Is Unknown Most people live in this state — moving forward without a perfect map. In that case, a few

### disciplines matter enormously. They are to

- 
- 
- 
- Build intellectual range: Read, learn, explore beyond your domain.

Follow momentum:Pay attention to where your energy and impact compound.

Avoid inertia:Comfort is the silent killer of potential.

Periodically reset direction: Every few years, reassess: Am I still climbing the right mountain?

- Strengthen the operating model: When the destination is unclear, the process becomes the strategy.

The Real Insight Your deep awareness or cognitive ability will help you determine if you are a goal person or a process person.

Sometimes, the craziness of your goals can let you define a goal and drive it with strong

### discipline. You can have goals to

- 
- 
- 
- 
- Build a $100M revenue product from zero Reduce Customer Acquisition cost by 80% Deliver 10x productivity per employee Launch a new business line generating 25% additional revenue Cut operational costs by 40% without reducing growth Or the other way is that your disciplined life, commitments and methods yield outcomes to reduce customer acquisition cost, generate 10x productivity or reduction of operational costs through your perseverance.

The second path is much easier and that is why leaders do not prefer defining BHAGs (Big Hairy Audacious Goals). They choose not to commit. So what path do you usually choose and will you define your BHAG for the near future or just exist?

title: Reinventing SaaS
content:
Anthropic triggered shock waves from the announcement of their AI capabilities inside modern software systems as part of their Mythos model: AI can now identify zero-day vulnerabilities across vast codebases and telemetry streams, detecting patterns that humans would rarely see. Instead of treating each exploit as an isolated event, these systems can cluster seemingly unrelated weaknesses into a single vulnerability family, revealing hidden attack surfaces much earlier. For major SaaS and software providers, this capability can dramatically accelerate product hardening and operational resilience. Adversaries getting access to such capabilities may employ the opposite story. This part of the world keeps changing and challenging the key players, especially the technology first companies. Where are we with this?

Imagine as the capabilities advance and in the near future say for example to book your uber, your smart interfaces have melted. You can book a taxi for your route using voice commands or text prompts - you will not require graphically intuitive applications or interfaces anymore. Quick rewind into the last 2 decades and you can see that software companies enjoyed something that most industries can only dream about: growth, margins, and predictability - all at the same time. These software companies have grown their toplines by 17% along with very high gross margins, compared to 6% growth rate of non-tech companies in S&P 500. Their future year(s) revenues are locked in at 80-95% for the large part.

The following table depicts the landscape of top 15 SaaS companies in the decreasing order of their annual revenues for the year 2025.

There are some idiosyncrasies to be found in them at the same time, at both ends of the spectrum - from the enviable top line growth to depressed or diluted valuations. Whether it is slowdown from the war effect, the AI game or normalization from unsustainable pandemic level growth, only for the second time since the global financial crisis, software companies are trading at a discount compared to the S&P500, at a P/E of 20 for software companies compared to 24 for S&P 500. Is this a temporary distraction? Here is a fundamental question to be answered in that case.

Should we be bullish or bearish of the Software companies? Is SaaS dead?

- Firstly, not all software companies are equal as their investments and strategic posture will define their story
- The chance of success or growth will also be determined by how these companies are embedded in business critical workflow, how they are part of the regulated environments and how their proprietary data format will make them more resilient. It is important to distinguish the User Interface from Business Processes and User Interface from the Proprietary Data formats
- Software companies, new or dated, with simple point solutions, generic data sets and low switching costs will be more vulnerable. They are usually represented by the ones that do not exist in the above top 15 list
- AI first narrative of these companies will expand their market rather than shrink them. Today’s global software spend at $1.4 Trillion is projected to become a $3 Trillion opportunity in the near future With that said, let’s look at the same list with a slightly different view. Companies with high AI focus are labeled in blue bubbles with the size of the bubble determining their gross margin. Companies not a leading edge AI focus represented with diamonds, X axis position indicating the revenue size and Y axis as the stickiness factor for their existing customers. Will there be displacement of some in this chart with new players? Will the UI/UX shift from graphically intuitive interactions to voice/prompt enabled access to data and business processes with increased determinism? Is SaaS at the cross-road for redefinition? Is the market capitalization moving towards $3 Trillion for the software industry? I have 4 Yeses. What do you think?

Charts courtesy of ChatGPT and Claude. Thoughts triggered from Anthropic Mythos Updates and from Thoma Bravo’s bold take on SaaS assessment.

title: Apocalypse of AI?
content:
AI Apocalypse or Cognitive Collapse? Choose Wisely.

Attention to ‘Commercial Media’, be it social, OTT, news, or published, compounds by spreading sadness and fear. Recent AI announcements are no different. The apocalyptic nature of these findings and announcements, whether for the benefits of good corporate citizens or adversaries, it certainly grabs attention. The frenzy around these major announcements and the speculation they create is concerning in itself.

How does Humankind and the so-called Machinekind (AI) intersect? Every tool humanity has ever built poses the same question: Not whether to use but when do we earn the rights to use? This applies to a calculator for calculations, social media for communications, the internet for knowledge, and now AI for acceleration. This concern necessitates examination of how and when both children and adults should use AI’s assistance. These tools will not replace thinking but will deteriorate thinking abilities if introduced prematurely.

AI models go through pretraining, then they are used in a generative capacity to solve problems or answer questions, and then it is expected that the human in the loop validates outcomes and provides reinforced learning as a feedback loop.

Similar to how AI models go through pretraining, humans must learn foundational skills, use AI’s aid for acceleration, and then validate with reinforced learning — for the benefit of both humans and machines.

With our greed to see the end game, we rush to use tools before building the mind. We optimize output before understanding the problem, or even before stating the problem in most cases. With this said, first principles are not optional, they are the only insurance against deteriorating thinking abilities.

Periodic Table of First Principles: Founding Elements to precede AI & most tools What are the first principles? When and how should they be taught or learnt? If you are an enthusiast scratching the surface of AI, coaching someone who needs to leverage AI, or working with a child and her developmental skills, here is a list of universal primitivesto be

### considered as the atomic building blocks

- Language & Communication Skills
- Numerical Intuition & Logic
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- Memorizing & Recalling Curiosity & Questioning Pattern Recognition Emotional Intelligence Ethics & Integrity Systems Thinking Creativity & Problem Solving Discipline & Focus Do a simple study to understand your personal scorecard on these principles, and if you are coaching a pupil, then her understanding of these.

Introducing principles first and then AI: Whether a child or a professional, if a tool replaces human thinking, delay its employment; and if it enhances thinking, introduce it — AI or not. For a Student This process is easy to relate to, as most of us have seen children growing around us:

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- Introducing Language & Curiosity (Birth – Age 5) Reading & Memorizing (Age 5 – 8) Arithmetic & Logic (Age 8 – 12) Research (Age 12 – 15) Systems Thinking & first introduction of AI (Age 15 – 18) Professional Specialization & Career Definition with full AI leverage (Age 18 – 24) Professional leverage of AI & judgment boosting with human in the loop (Age 24+) Introduction of AI, social media, and other tools must be done carefully. You cannot write an essay when the thinking was never done. The child may enter the university but will never be able to perform.

For the Enterprise / Professionals An executive creating a strategy with AI without thinking is no different than a 14-year-old using AI to write an essay. Organizations that build the cognitive immune system first — with first-principles thinking, rigorous questioning, and human accountability — will see meaningful amplification in long-term business value.

### Within organizations

- Humans must own consequential decisions based on the options AI provides
- AI output should be treated as a draft that requires human validation
- Speed cannot be used in isolation — there are consequences from blind reliance on AI

### Ask a simple question every time

Is cognition being replaced by the tool — or amplified by it?

title: Yet Another LLM: The Leadership Language Model
content:
Have you heard of Virgin Atlantic and the Virgin Group? Richard Branson started with discounted mail order music sales, then Virgin Records in 1972. Success in music funded Virgin Atlantic in 1984, followed by telecom, banking, hotels and eventually Virgin Galactic. Over five decades, Branson repeatedly disrupted industries with poor customer experience through branding, simplicity and customer obsession.

Of course, we all know of IKEA. A big thank you to Ingvar Kamprad for giving a lot of men, and some women, a sense of accomplishment from assembling furnishing units which arrive in flat packaging through standardized manufacturing and self service warehousing. What looks simple today is one of the most scalable retail systems in the world because the business model was built around operational simplicity and affordability.

Customer obsession demonstrated by Steve Jobs’ leadership and a series of Apple products is second to none. He transformed personal technology into something intuitive, emotional and beautifully designed. Jobs built extraordinary teams of engineers, designers and product thinkers while communicating a vision so clearly that the organization aligned around simplicity and customer obsession.

Similarly, Elon Musk picked industries that most people considered impossible to disrupt. Electric vehicles, reusable rockets and commercial space infrastructure were viewed as financially irrational ideas. Yet Tesla and SpaceX scaled because Musk recruited highly technical talent and aligned them around missions larger than products themselves. You can add more names to this lineup, including Bill Gates, Walt Disney, Charles Schwab, John Chambers, Tommy Hilfiger and many more.

What is fascinating is that these leaders were dyslexic, autistic or demonstrated strong neurodivergent characteristics. Dyslexic and autistic minds are often forced to compensate differently. They become highly verbal communicators. They simplify complexity because complexity becomes exhausting. They delegate because they cannot and do not want to control every operational detail. They focus on patterns, systems, customers and outcomes rather than procedural perfection. In many cases, they stop trying to become the smartest functional expert in the room and instead become orchestrators of talent, energy and mission. So, here is a take on the success of these leaders and the model that largely attaches to their ways of working, despite their implied handicap .

1. Firstly, they identify problems that genuinely matter before they obsess over solutions. They begin with a meaningful customer or societal frustration. Amazon solved convenience. IKEA solved affordability. Tesla solved sustainable transportation. 2. They recruit people smarter than themselves. They become talent magnets instead of trying to be the best coder, accountant or operator.

3. They communicate until the mission becomes contagious. Great organizations are built on repeated communication. Jobs repeatedly spoke about simplicity and customer experience, and Musk speaks about interplanetary civilization as examples. They created emotional conviction around a mission internally and externally.

4. They become obsessed with customer value. Customers care about outcomes and product experiences. Faster delivery. Better products. Lower cost. Simpler experiences. Leaders who remain close to customer pain points build enduring organizations. 5. They think in terms of 10x possibilities. Incremental thinking creates incremental companies, keeping you constrained in operational limitations and repeatedly patting your own back. Radical thinking creates category defining organizations. Netflix questioned television distribution. Airbnb questioned hotels. SpaceX questioned rocket economics.

6. They delegate execution but own direction. They delegate and do not micromanage. They own clarity of direction, priorities, talent quality and decision making velocity while teams execute independently.

How does this apply to me?

This is a call out to operate like the CEO of your domain. Whether you are a manager, architect, recruiter, product owner or functional leader, stop behaving like someone merely completing assigned tasks. Start thinking about customers, scale, efficiency, communication, talent and long term value creation. That mindset transition is often what separates managers from leaders.

So, what are you? Just introspect.
